9224 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON VEHICULAR TECHNOLOGY, VOL. 74, NO. 6, JUNE 2025
Optimizing Hybrid RIS-Aided ISAC Systems in V2X
Networks: A Deep Reinforcement Learning Method
for Anti-Eavesdropping Techniques
Yu Ya o
, Member, IEEE, Zhixing Zhu, Pu Miao , Member, IEEE, Xu Cheng, Senior Member, IEEE,
Feng Shu
, Member, IEEE, and Jiangzhou Wang , Fellow, IEEE
Abstract—Physical layer security (PLS) technique is expected
to play a crucial part in the vehicle-to-everything (V2X) net-
works, by offering secure transmission to protect conﬁdential
information from potential eavesdropper. Considering a hybrid
active-passive reconﬁgurable intelligent surfaces (RISs)-enhanced
integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) system, this paper
proposes a novel secure scheme for transmitting conﬁdential in-
formation and performing radar sensing, where vehicle-to-vehicle
(V2V) links share the spectrum resource preoccupied by vehicle-to-
infrastructure (V2I) links. We aim to optimize the sum secrecy rate
of V2I links by jointly designing the transmit beamforming of RSU,
the radio spectrum reuse scheme of V2X links, and active and pas-
sive reﬂection beamforming of hybrid RIS. With above optimiza-
tion, the proposed approach can enhance secure communication
performance of V2I links while guaranteeing the communication
quality of V2V links and target sensing capacity of RSU. Since the
system model is dynamic, and it is difﬁcult to handle the nonconvex
problem, an efﬁcient hierarchical twin delayed deep deterministic
policy gradient (HTD3) method is developed to learn the secure
beamforming and spectrum sharing strategies against potential
eavesdropping. The proposed method decomposes the spectrum
allocation into the deep Q-network procedure and designs the
secure beamforming variables by employing the TD3 algorithm.
Numerical results exhibit that given a sufﬁcient power budget of
hybrid RIS, our HTD3-based method enhances both the secure
Received 29 June 2024; revised 28 October 2024; accepted 13 January 2025.

Hainan University under Grant KYQD(ZR)-21008, in part by the Collaborative
Innovation Center of Information Technology, Hainan University under Grant
XTCX2022XXC07, and in part by the National Key Research and Development
Program of China under Grant 2023YFF0612900. The review of this article was
coordinated by Dr. Muhammad Ismail. (Corresponding author: Yu Yao.)

Zhixing Zhu is with the School of Information Engineering, East China Jiao-
tong University, Nanchang 330013, China (e-mail: zxingchuh@ecjtu.edu.cn).
Pu Miao is with the School of Electronic and Information Engineering,
Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China (e-mail: mpvae@qdu.edu.cn).
Xu Cheng is with the School of Electronics and Communication
Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Shenzhen 510275, China (e-mail:
chengx95@mail.sysu.edu.cn).
Feng Shu is with the School of Information and Communication Engineering,
Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China, and also with the School of Elec-
tronic and Optical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology,
Nanjing 210094, China (e-mail: shufeng0101@163.com).
Jiangzhou Wang is with the School of Engineering, University of Kent, CT2
7NT Canterbury, U.K. (e-mail: j.z.wang@kent.ac.uk).
Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/TVT.2025.3538471
communication performance of V2I links and radar detection
capability of RSU compared with the existing learning methods.
Index Terms—Vehicular network, deep reinforcement
learning, integrated sensing and communication (ISAC), secure
communication, hybrid active-passive reconﬁgurable intelligent
surface.
I. INTRODUCTION
D
RIVEN by the rapid advancement of wireless communi-
cation techniques, vehicle-to-everything (V2X) networks,
are new scheme to provide leading edge applications, for exam-
ple vehicular entertainment and intelligent driving
[1], [2].To
implement these applications, intelligent vehicles are required
to have communication and sensing capabilities. Therefore, it
is important to deploy integrated sensing and communication
(ISAC) system in mobile V2X networks for high rate communi-
cation and high-precision positioning
[3]. ISAC systems have
the ability to realize multi-view sensing information sharing
among intelligent vehicles and roadside units (RSUs), enhancing
the security and efﬁciency of mobile vehicular communication
networks
[4]. Since the ISAC waveforms also carry private
information for the secure users, the radar target, as a malicious
wiretapper, for example, an illegitimate user, might intercept
the communication data intended for secure users
[5].Inthe
V2X networks, conﬁdentiality of communication data must be
ensured since the disclosure of user information may result
in severe consequences, for example illegal tracking, property
damage, and trafﬁc accidents
[6]. For this purpose, physical layer
security (PLS) techniques are needed for the dual functional op-
eration in security-critical V2X communication system designs.
To address the potential security problem in ISAC systems,
in
[7], the multiple input multiple output (MIMO) system was
devised to convey a mixture of two different waveforms, con-
taining communication information for the authorized receivers
and artiﬁcial noise (AN) to disturb the eavesdropper (Eve), both
of which can be employed for radar target detection. In
[8],
the constructive interference (CI) method was used to optimize
the dual-function waveform for utilizing the intrinsic multiuser
interference (MUI) as beneﬁcial power to secure ISAC systems.
The AN and CI methods were jointly used to optimize secure
performance under the hypothesis of imperfect channel state
information (CSI)
[9], which was shown to outperform the tra-
ditional AN-assisted security design. The presence of malicious
0018-9545 © 2025 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, and training of artiﬁcial intelligence and similar technologies.
Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on September 09,2025 at 07:02:22 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
YAO et al.: OPTIMIZING HYBRID RIS-AIDED ISAC SYSTEMS IN V2X NETWORKS 9225
eavesdroppers is considered in ISAC-based V2X network, and
a power control scheme was proposed to guarantee secure
communication [10]. To improve the vehicular communication
performance against smart eavesdropper, a novel resource al-
location strategy was developed to improve the system secrecy
rate while ensuring radar sensing requirements
[11].
Recently, reconﬁgurable intelligent surface (RIS)
[12],asa
promising technique, has been developed to enhance the spec-
trum efﬁciency and satisfy the PSL of V2X communications.
RIS is capable of reducing high path loss in high-frequency
bands to improve vehicular network connectivity as well as ob-
tain performance gains by providing programmable propagation
environment
[13], [14]. The deployment of RIS in enhancing the
quality of service (QoS) of vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) com-
munications was conﬁrmed in
[15]. The work of [16] veriﬁed
the improvement of security and reliability in V2X communica-
tions with the help of the RIS. The effectiveness of employing
RIS-aided unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) in V2X networks
was presented in
[17]. To optimize the performance of simul-
taneous transmitting and reﬂecting reconﬁgurable intelligent
surface (STAR-RIS) in vehicular communication networks, a
novel scheme is to deploy STAR-RIS on the surface of intelligent
vehicle to enhance sensing and communication capabilities
[18].
ISAC systems have the problem of information leakage when
employing the dual-function waveforms to sense the environ-
ment. RIS-assisted beamforming design methods have been
proposed for PLS improvement in the ISAC systems
[19], [20],
[21], [22]. The secure performance was optimized by jointly
devising the beamforming at the base station (BS) and the
reﬂection weights at the active RIS, subject to the radar detection
performance requirement as well as the power budget constraints
at the BS and active RIS
[19]. Assuming that an Eve possibly
occurs in a speciﬁc location and the eavesdropping channel is
imperfect known, an optimization problem was established to
optimize the system secrecy rate of the authorized user
[20].
The authors studied a RIS-assisted ISAC secure system where
BS concurrently executes multi-user multi-input single-output
(MU-MISO) secure transmission and senses a potential eaves-
dropping target
[21]. By jointly devising the transmit beam-
forming, the artiﬁcial noise (AN) waveforms and the passive
RIS weights, secure transmission performance was optimized
in
[22]. Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) procedure was used
to obtain the optimal learning scheme.
Owing to the passive reﬂection characteristics of the RIS, the
employment of RIS would cause the multiplicative path loss
effect, thus resulting in the serious communication and sensing
performance deterioration. The fully active RIS is capable of uti-
lizing ampliﬁers to tackle the serious channel attenuation. How-
ever, it requires extra power consumption to improve the strength
of the reﬂected waveforms and noise at the active RIS
[23].To
overcome the aforementioned issues, hybrid active-passive RIS
including a certain number of active units was proposed
[24],
[25], which serves the dual purpose of reﬂecting and amplifying
incoming signals and achieves a signiﬁcant improvement of
system performance. The robust beamforming optimization for
a hybrid RIS-enabled ISAC system was proposed in
[24].The
work of [25] developed a design scheme to optimize the date rate
for hybrid RIS-assisted communication system that operates in
non-line-of sight (NLoS) channels. The jointly design of the
hybrid precoders and the hybrid RIS weights for optimizing the
weighted sum data rate, subject to the overall system power
constraint, was proposed in [26]. The authors of [15] also
considered adding active reﬂection units to passive RIS and then
employed the hybrid architecture for energy transmission, and
veriﬁed that the hybrid RIS can provide superior performance
than fully passive RIS.
Compared with the traditional optimization algorithms, the
artiﬁcial intelligence (AI) approaches have revealed superior ro-
bustness to handle complex problems with inaccurate modeling,
multiuser couplings and uncertain dynamics
[27], [28], [29],
[30].In[31], the deep Q-learning (DQN) technique was em-
ployed to realize secure beamforming in the presence of potential
eavesdropper. DQN belongs to a deep learning scheme that cal-
culates the system state and is developed to handle these discrete
action problems. Furthermore, the authors of
[32] and [27] de-
veloped DRL method to handle the continuous control problem,
and the reﬂecting beamforming was designed efﬁciently. In
[33],
a DRL-based technique was proposed to allocate the spectrum
resource for the relay-aided cellular V2X communications. Dif-
ferent from centralized training strategy, multi-agent DRL ap-
proach is capable of compromising cooperative and competitive
trade-offs of agents to realize a dynamic balance in mobile
vehicular networks
[34]. Since the consideration of intelligent
resource allocation makes the RIS-aided orthogonal frequency
division multiplexing (OFDM) system more complex, a twin
delayed deep deterministic policy gradient (TD3)-based tech-
nique
[35], [36] was developed to enhance learning efﬁciency.
The work of [19] proposed a DRL-based strategy to handle the
joint optimization problem of radar sensing and communication
in the STAR-RIS-enhanced vehicular networks. However, these
studies
[19], [27], [29], [30], [33], [34], [36] only considered
to optimize the achievable transmission capacity without tak-
ing into account the secure communication and outdated CSI
condition in the system models. To the best of our knowledge,
PLS optimization technique based on DRL method for hybrid
RIS-enhanced secure V2X networks has not been studied before.
Motivated by the above discussions, we study the PLS of hy-
brid RIS-enhanced ISAC secure system in realistic time-varying
V2X networks. In particular, assuming that the malicious target
might intercept the private information intended to the cellular
vehicle users (CVUs), a novel hierarchical DRL-based technique
is proposed to design an efﬁcient scheme for securing V2X
networks under the condition of outdated CSI. An inspection into
two representative DRL techniques, for instance, DQN and TD3,
displays that they depend on the double Q-network framework.
The system architecture brings tight coupling between double
Q-networks and thus causes performance loss. The phenomenon
motivates us to propose a hierarchical TD3 (called HTD3)
procedure to enhance the learning efﬁciency. The main idea
of the HTD3 is to minimize the action space by introducing
a hierarchical framework, namely, the discrete variables are
designed by the DQN procedure while the continuous variables
are optimized by TD3 algorithm. Our main contributions of this
paper are summarized as follows.
r
We are the ﬁrst to adopt hybrid RIS-enhanced ISAC
into secure vehicular communication networks with high
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on September 09,2025 at 07:02:22 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
9226 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON VEHICULAR TECHNOLOGY, VOL. 74, NO. 6, JUNE 2025
mobility, where multiple V2V links reuse the radio spec-
trum already occupied by V2I links. In addition, the outage
probability is employed to guarantee the reliability of hy-
brid RIS-enhanced V2V links.
r
We investigate the sum V2I secrecy rate maximization
problem, where the transmit beamforming of RSU, the
spectrum reuse scheme of V2X links, and active and pas-
sive reﬂection weights of hybrid RIS are jointly designed
while ensuring the V2V communication quality require-
ment and desired RSU target sensing capability under a
particular resource constraint.
r
Since the system model is highly dynamic, an efﬁcient
HTD3 learning algorithm is proposed to obtain the op-
timal anti-eavesdropping strategy, where the outer-loop
DQN procedure can quickly obtain the optimal spectrum
allocation, the inner-loop TD3 procedure has the ability to
optimize the secure beamforming variables.
r
Simulation results are offered to validate that the hy-
brid RIS-enhanced V2X network improves the anti-
eavesdropping communication performance compared to
conventional RIS-based systems. Moreover, the achieved
numerical results conﬁrm that the employment of hybrid
RIS in time-varying vehicular networks can compensate
for the channel losses owing to the mobility of connected
vehicles. Furthermore, the convergence and efﬁciency of
the proposed method are also demonstrated.
The rest of this article is organized as follows: Section
II in-
troduces the anti-eavesdropping model in hybrid RIS-enhanced
vehicular networks. In Section
III, the joint beamforming opti-
mization and spectrum allocation problem is proposed, and the
HTD3 learning procedure is designed to handle the formulated
DRL problem. Section
IV offers simulation experiments to
conﬁrm the advantage of the devised HTD3 learning procedure.
Finally, some conclusions are presented in Section
V.

case and lowercase letters signify matrices and vectors, respec-

proper size which all the elements are equal to one and zero
respectively. E signiﬁes the expectation operator. ∇(·) signiﬁes
the ﬁrst-order partial derivative. The distribution of a complex
Gaussian with zero mean and variance σ
2
is represented by
CN(0,σ
2
).
II. SYSTEM MODEL
We consider a hybrid RIS-enhanced vehicular network with
V2I and V2V links that offers support for intelligent driving
services, such as security information sharing and environmental
perception, as described in Fig.
1. In particular, the studied V2X
network consists of M single antenna cellular-vehicle users
(CVUs) that communicate with the multi-antenna ISAC-based
RSU using V2I links, and L pairs of device-to-device vehicle
users (DVUs) that connect with each other using V2V links.
Furthermore, a malicious radar target acts as an Eve, and uti-
lizes single antenna for intercepting the conﬁdential information
Fig. 1. A hybrid RIS-enhanced secure V2X network based on ISAC
framework.
transmission between the RSU and CVUs. The RSU equipped
with N
t
transmitting and N
r
receiving antennas, synchronously
serves M CVUs and detects an Eve by emitting an ISAC probing
waveform.

A. Channel Model
We consider Mode 4 described in the cellular vehicular net-
work framework, where connected vehicle users have a spectrum
resource pool that they can choose V2V transmission indepen-
dently

, are similarly deﬁned.
In the considered vehicular network, it is difﬁcult for RSU
and hybrid RIS to gain perfect CSI owing to the mobility of
the vehicle and the existence of transmission and processing
delay
[39], [40]. As a result, CSI may be outdated when RSU
and hybrid RIS convey information to CVUs. If the outdated
CSI is used for secure beamforming, it would result in an
adverse impact on the demodulation at the CVUs, thus gen-
erating signiﬁcant performance degradation
[40]. Hence, it is
appropriate to analyze outdated CSI in the hybrid RIS-enhanced
V2X network.
2
Let T
delay
signify the time delay between the
outdated CSI and the actual CSI. Therefore, the relationship
between the outdated channel power gain h(t) and the actual
channel power gain h(t + T
delay
) is given by
h (t + T
delay
)=ρ

) obey a zero mean Gaussian dis-
tribution. ρ
d
(0 <ρ
d
≤ 1) denotes the outdated autocorrelation
coefﬁcient, which is expressed by ρ
d
= J
0
(2πf
D
T
delay
), with
J
0
(·) and f
D
being the zeroth-order Bessel function and the
Doppler spread, respectively. Notice that ρ
d
= 1 suggests the
delay effect does not exist, whereas ρ
d
= 0 signiﬁes no CSI.
2
It is worth noting that the outdated CSI of all channels can be achieved by ex-
isting channel estimation methods. It is appropriate to suppose that the outdated
CSI of Eve is known in the RIS-enhanced V2X network when the Eve is an active
user but untrusted by all authorized users
[41]. Under these assumptions, our
analysis provides information on the best secure communication performance
upper bounds for more complex scenarios.
B. Communications Model
We begin with the signals emitted from the RSU. The dual
function transmit beam signal at the RSU in time step n is
denoted as

is a wide-sense stationary random
process with zero-mean and unit power, and uncorrelated with

cation symbols are uncorrelated with the radar waveform, i.e,

Active V2V links reuse the spectrum resource of the V2I
links, hence mutual interference should be properly considered
in communication model. In this case, when the RSU sends
sensitive information to the mth CVU, the mth CVU receives
the signal transmitted from its connected RSU, the reﬂected
signal from the hybrid RIS and the interference from the V2V
transmitter occupied by the mth V2I link, which is expressed as

C. Radar Model
As described in Fig.
13, the dual function waveform illu-
minates the radar target (i.e., Eve) through a direct path and
reﬂected paths, and be backscattered to the RSU via all the
transmission channels. The target echo is achieved by trans-
mitting the dual function waveform via four various channels,
i.e., RSU-Eve-RSU, RSU-RIS-Eve-RSU, RSU-Eve-RIS-RSU
and RSU-RIS-Eve-RIS-RSU. Hence, the radar signal received
at the RSU, which is composed of the target echo, the V2V
interference signal and the additive noise, given by
(14) shown
at the bottom of this page, where H

, H

, H

and H

denote
the target response matrices. They can be modeled as LOS and
depend on azimuth angles
[42], respectively, given by

in contrast to other metrics, MI has a more brief structure,
which is helpful to our analysis of the inﬂuence of uncertainty
information on radar sensing performance and the design of
algorithms

D. Problem Formulation
As explained in the preceding section, each V2I link is pri-
marily utilized for the efﬁcient transmission of security-critical
information. Hence, we focus on optimizing the sum V2I se-
crecy rate, that is,

M
m=1
R
S
m
, while guaranteeing RSU sensing
capability. Furthermore, the V2V transmission reliability is the
key performance index of interest. The outage probability is
employed to guarantee the reliability of V2V communications,
given by
P
r

R
l
≤ R

, (21)
where R
l

denotes the minimum data rate of the lth V2V
link, P
outage
max
signiﬁes the maximum allowable outage probabil-
ity. From this foundation, the secure vehicular communication
problem includes the joint optimization of the communication
and radar beamforming vectors {v
m
}
M
m=1
, w
r
, the passive and
active reﬂection matrices Φ, Ψ, the spectrum allocation scheme
for the V2X links ρ = {ρ

, ∀m, l}. Hence, the problem that
guarantees the desired transmission security and quality for
hybrid RIS-enhanced vehicular networks while providing radar
sensing capability, is cast as

where P
R
max
and P
RIS
max
signify the maximum power budgets of
the RSU and the hybrid RIS, respectively. MI
min
denotes the
given radar MI threshold, R
m
min
is the minimum data rate of the
mth V2I link. To elaborate further,
(22c) illustrates the spectrum
allocation strategy for the V2X links, that is, the frequency spec-
trum of one CVU is shared with at most one V2V link, whereas
one V2V link only shares the spectrum of at most one V2I link.
The constraint in
(22d) is considered to secure a particular radar
detection criterion.
(22e) and (22f) illustrate the constraints of
active and passive RIS reﬂection units, respectively. (22g) is set
to satisfy the worst-case data rate required for each V2I link.
III. DRL BASED SECURE BEAMFORMING AND SPECTRUM
ALLOCATION
The resulting problem
(22) is not easy to handle since it is
non-convex and the variables are coupled with each other. More-
over, in hybrid RIS-enhanced secure V2X network, the channel
conditions would vary dynamically. In addition, problem
(22)
belongs to single time slot optimization problem. Nevertheless,
the conventional optimization procedures might converge to a
non-optimal feasible solution since the historical state and the
long term beneﬁt are ignored. As a result, it is impracticable
to exploit the conventional optimization procedures to acquire
a valid secure communication strategy in dynamic vehicular
communication network.
A. Environmental Model
DRL is regarded as a dynamic programming tool which is em-
ployed to handle the sophisticated control problem by training
the optimized strategy in complex systems [44]. On this basis,
we recast the secure communication performance optimization
problem as a Markov decision process (MDP) framework so
that the model-free DRL procedure can be exploited. In DRL
framework, the secure V2X network is considered as the external
environment, the central control unit at the hybrid RIS-enhanced
RSU is viewed as the learning agent. The system state, system
action, and reward function for the secure vehicular communi-
cation problem are described as follows.
System State: Let S represent the state space. The state s
(t)
∈
S at the current time t includes spectrum allocation, hybrid
RIS weights, transmit beamforming vectors, radar MI, data rate
of all CVUs and DVUs, and secrecy rate of all CVUs at the
previous time t − 1, as well as the outdated channel conditions
corresponding to all C RIS-enhanced V2X links at the current
time t. Hence, the system state s
(t)
is given by
s
(t)
=
⎧
⎨
⎩
a
(t−1)
,

(R
m
min
)
(t−1)

m∈M
,


R
l
min



l∈L
,

h
(t)
c

⎬

, (23)
where a
(t−1)
is the action at previous time t − 1 including
spectrum allocation, hybrid RIS weights, transmit beamforming
vectors, radar MI, data rate of all CVUs and DVUs, and secrecy
rate of all CVUs. (R
m
min
)
(t−1)
and (R
l
min
)
(t−1)
are the minimum
date rates of the mth CVU and the lth DVU at time step t − 1,
respectively.
System Action: Let A represent the system action space.
The learning agent selects the transmit beamforming vectors
{v
m
}
M
m=1
, w
r
at the RSU, the passive and active reﬂection
weights Φ, Ψ at the hybrid RIS, the coefﬁcients of bandwidth
resources allocated for V2X links ρ. Therefore, the system action
at time step t is described as
a
(t)
=


v
(t)
m

, w

Except the binary spectrum sharing variables ρ
(t)
, all other
secure beamforming variables {{v
(t)
m
}
M
m=1
, w
(t)
r
, Φ

, Ψ
(t)
}
are continuous variables. The feasibility of the system action
depends on the constraints of the hybrid RIS-enhanced secure
vehicular communication optimization problem in
(22).
Reward function: The reward function serves as an indicator
to assess how well the secure vehicular communication policy
is when the hybrid RIS-enhanced RSU performs a valid action.
The secure communication performance is enhanced when the
reward signal at training phase correlates with the predetermined
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on September 09,2025 at 07:02:22 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
9230 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON VEHICULAR TECHNOLOGY, VOL. 74, NO. 6, JUNE 2025
goal. Hence, it is essential to devise a valid reward to enhance
the vehicular communication security and quality. In our work,
the reward function signiﬁes the optimal object, and our aim
is to optimize the sum secrecy rate of all CVUs while ensuring
all legitimate vehicle users’ communication quality requirement
and RSU’s target detection performance. Therefore, the security-
critical reward function is written as
r =

(28)
In
(25), the part 1 signiﬁes the system utility, the part 2 signiﬁes
the cost function which is characterized as the satisfactory
detection performance, the part 3 and the part 4 denote the cost
functions which are represented as the acceptable communica-
tion quality constraints of V2I and V2V links, respectively. The
parameters λ
1
, λ
2
and λ
3
represent the positive values of the parts
2-4 in
(25), respectively, which are employed to trade-off the cost
and utility
[45]. The purposes of (26), (27) and (28) are to enforce
the minimum detection performance and the minimum data rate
constraints, respectively. If these requirements are fulﬁlled in
the current time step, that is, b
RAD
= 1, b
V2I
m
= 1orb
V2V
l
= 1,
signifying that there is an encouragement of the reward function
owing to the desired detection performance or transmission
quality guarantees.
The objective of the training agent is to ﬁnd an optimal
policy π
∗
that optimizes the long-term discounted reward. The
cumulative discounted reward is given by
R
t
=
N

, (29)
with γ ∈ (0, 1] signifying the discount factor. For a given π,
Q-function with (s, a) at time slot t is denoted by
Q
π
(s

)=E

B. Hierarchical Integration of DQN and TD3
Considering the fact that the secure performance optimization
problem in
(22) is a mixed-integer problem with continuous and
discrete control variables, we develop a hierarchical learning
framework, as presented in Fig.
2, where the DQN and TD3
techniques are employed to enable the agent to train faster in
time-varying hybrid RIS-enhanced V2X network. In particular,
the binary discrete variable ρ
ml
decides the spectrum alloca-
tion strategy for the V2X links, while the continuous variables
Fig. 2. HTD3-based anti-eavesdropping strategy for hybrid RIS-enhanced
V2X network.
contain the RSU’s transmit beamforming {v
m
}
M
m=1
, w
r
and
the hybrid RIS’s passive and active reﬂection matrices Φ, Ψ.
The fundamental principle of the hierarchical training scheme
is to divide the system action space into discrete part and
continuous part, namely (a
d
,a
c
). The discrete part can be
learned employing the traditional DQN algorithm. Speciﬁed
the discrete part a
d
= {ρ
ml
}
m∈M,l∈L
in the outer-loop network
architecture, we emphasize on the optimized solutions a
c
=
{{v
m
}
M
m=1
, w
r
, Φ, Ψ} to a continuous control task in
(22),
which are solved by the inner-loop TD3 procedure. The goal
of TD3 technique
[35] is to address the function approximation
errors that would produce the poor state resulting in subopti-
mal solutions in DDPG. The HTD3 training scheme permits
us to separate the combinatorial optimization of the spectrum
allocation strategy from the optimization of continuous secure
beamforming variables. The convergence point of the HTD3
procedure is regarded as the Q-value of the DQN algorithm.
The advantage of the devised HTD3 scheme primarily lies in
the decreased system action space, hence it has the ability to
enhance the training capability.
In the proposed hierarchical learning procedure, the outer-
loop DQN is used to calculate the discrete Q-function Q(s, a
d
)
employing the value estimation Q(s, a
d
; θ), with θ being the
deep neural network (DNN) weight. The purpose of DQN
procedure is to minimize a loss function at current time
step, i.e.,
L(θ
t
)=E

) − Q(s

referred to as temporal-difference error, i.e.,
δ

The weight θ can be obtained by taking the partial differentiation
of Eq.
(31) with respect to θ, that is,
θ
t+1
= θ
t

), (33)
where β denotes the learning rate of the Q-network θ. Therefore,
the policy π
t
(s) of the outer-loop DQN procedure is expressed
as
π

The TD3 technique is a modiﬁed DDPG procedure, which is also
used to deal with continuous action space. We ﬁrst introduce the
DDPG procedure
[46]. In the course of the learning, the weights
of the DNNs can be trained as follows:
1) Updating of the Critic Network: Consider that a mini-
batch of N
B
transition tuples is sampled from the experience
replay buffer D. The target Q-value of the ith transition tuple
(s
i

y

.
2) Updating of the Actor Network: In order to enhance the
policy offered by the devised scheme, the weights of the actor
network θ
µ
is trained to let the action output from the actor
network be in the direction of increasing the Q value. The
weights of the actor network θ
µ
is trained employing gradient
ascent method, i.e.,
θ

), β
µ
signiﬁes the learning rate of θ
µ
.
3) Updating of the Target Networks: The soft update tech-
nique
[47] is employed to train the weights of the target critic
network θ
Q
′
, so that the target value V (s; θ
Q
′
) changes gently
in the process of learning, thus ensuring the stability of learning.
Therefore, the weight coefﬁcients θ
Q
′
can be trained using
Poylak averaging method with coefﬁcient τ, i.e.,
θ
Q
′
= τθ
Q
+(1 − τ)θ
Q
′
, 0 <τ ≪ 1. (39)
Analogously, the weights of the target actor network θ
µ
′
can be
trained, i.e.,
θ
µ
′
= τθ
µ
+(1 − τ)θ
µ
′
, 0 <τ ≪ 1. (40)
To guarantee reasonable exploration of the continuous action
spaces, a general exploration strategy is adopted for the agent
policy. Speciﬁcally, the action is chosen from a random proce-
dure, which is considered as a Gaussian distribution with mean
µ(s; θ
µ
) and variance υσ
2
, with υ being a factor to attenuate the
randomness of the learning procedure. where υ is a exploration
noise and σ is a decay constant.
However, DDPG technique has the bias overestimation prob-
lem, which would result in an accumulation error. Speciﬁcally,
the error generates the deteriorated state to admit a large Q
value producing a nonoptimal policy. The main objective of
inner-loop TD3 technique is to reduce the function approxi-
mation errors that produce overestimated value estimates and
nonoptimal strategies in DDPG procedure
[48]. The inner-loop
TD3 framework consists of three networks, containing an actor
network and two critic networks. In which, the actor network
contains two parts: 1) policy network with DNN weights θ
µ
and 2) target policy network with DNN weights θ
µ
′
, and a
critic network consists of two parts: 1) main Q network with
DNN weights θ
Q
and 2) target Q network with DNN weights
θ
Q
′
, which decouple target Q value computation and action
selection. TD3 utilizes several supplementary schemes to DDPG
procedure to avoid overestimation of the value functions as
follows:
1) Clipped Double Q-learning: TD3 procedure suppresses
persistently the bias overestimation by choosing the
smaller target Q value, just as DDQN procedure utilizes
double Q-learning technique to address the bias overesti-
mation problem. The TD3 procedure chooses the smaller
value function between two actor networks, i.e.,
y
i
= r (s
i
,a
c
i
)+γ min
j=1,2

Q
′

s
i+1
,µ
′
(s
i+1
)| θ
Q
′
j

.
(41)
2) Delayed Policy Updates: The scheme gradually trains
policy network after training the critic network precisely
in preparation for inaccurate value functions if policy
training can not work validly. When the critical network
is trained twice, the policy and target networks need to be
learned once. Furthermore, TD3 utilizes delayed policy
updates to reduce the growth of error.
3) Target Policy Smoothing: DDPG procedure may generate
an overestimation of the value function of the policy when
a peak appears in achieving the policy from the actor
network. A smoothing regularization scheme for the Q-
function can prevent the policy network from overﬁtting.
TD3 procedure is capable of ameliorating the issue by
appropriately smoothing the target policy via the clipped
random noise addition to the action, that is,
a
c
i+1
= µ
′
(s
i+1
)+[ε]
c
−c
, (42)
where ε represents a Gaussian random variable with dis-
tribution ε ∼CN(0,σ
2
s
) and c denotes a noise constant.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on September 09,2025 at 07:02:22 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
9232 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON VEHICULAR TECHNOLOGY, VOL. 74, NO. 6, JUNE 2025
The clipped random noise is viewed as a regularization
method that smoothes the Q-values within a limited range
[−c, c] around the action µ
′
(s
i+1
).
Based on the above deﬁnitions and discussions, the proposed
HTD3-based secure communication performance optimization
algorithm for the hybrid RIS-enhanced V2X network is summa-
rized in Algorithm
1. The central control unit at the RSU gathers
V2X network information and optimizes secure beamforming
and spectrum resource allocation. During the learning phase,
the central control unit initializes all neural network weights
as well as gathers the current state containing the CSI of all
vehicles, previous radar MI, secrecy rate of all CVUs, data
rate of all CVUs and DVUs. The system state is input into the
HTD3 framework to train the network parameters. Speciﬁed
the state, the outer-loop DQN procedure determines the binary
spectrum allocation indicator ρ
ml
for each V2X link, and then
the channel coefﬁcients are updated based on the spectrum
sharing protocol. The discrete action a
d
is selected by using
the probability distribution π
θ
(s, a
d
). By maximizing the se-
cure communication performance of all V2I links, the neural
networks of the inner TD3 produce the continuous beamforming
variables and the value estimate. Speciﬁcally, in each time slot,
the proposed inner-loop TD3 procedure estimates continuous
action a
c
from the current actor network µ(s; θ
µ
), adds the
noise and computes the immediate reward. HTD3 procedure
updates channel information at each time step and next system
state accordingly. A replay buffer with the size D is utilized
for learning the actor and critic networks, and initialized at the
beginning. To be precise, HTD3 stores the environmental model
data into the replay buffer, and extracts a small batch of data
from the buffer to learn the parameters of the actor networks
and critic networks.
The learning phase requires a high performance computing
server which is implemented ofﬂine at the RSU while the exe-
cution phase is accomplished online. The neural network param-
eters need to be trained only when the external environment has
experienced signiﬁcantly varies, primarily relying on the chan-
nel dynamics and communication and sensing requirements.
After effective learning in Algorithm
1, the HTD3 framework
is loaded for the execution phase. In the execution phase, the
central control unit employs the learned HTD3 framework to
output its chosen action using the network parameters, with
the obtained state from the hybrid RIS-enhanced secure V2X
network. Then, the considered V2X network feeds back an
immediate reward value and a current state to the hierarchical
learning agent. Finally, the RSU’s transmit beamforming vec-
tors, the hybrid RIS’s active and passive reﬂection weights and
spectrum allocation strategy are attained based on the selected
action.
C. Computational Complexity
To assess the computational complexity of the proposed hi-
erarchical algorithm, the ﬂoating-point operations per second
(FPOS) is employed as the metric in the training phase. For any
fully connected layer L
i
of input size I
i
(which is proportional
to the number of system state) and output size O
i
, the number of

The policy implemented in this paper has 2 hidden layers of
sizes 258 and 128. The number of FPOS during the inference is
mainly determined by the matrix multiplications of the policy
network, which has four layers with size |S|, 256, 128, and |A|.
The amount of FPOS for a connected layer is twice the product of
the size of the hidden layers. That is, speciﬁed a hidden layer of
size 128 that has a previous layer of size 256, the weight matrix
should be of size 128 × 256 and the FPOS is 2 × (128 × 256).
According to the aforementioned analysis, the computational
complexity of the proposed HTD3 algorithm is

It shows that the complexity of the devised hierarchical pro-
cedure is higher than the traditional DQN learning technique.
Nevertheless, our hierarchical procedure realizes superior per-
formance than that of the traditional DQN technique, which will
be presented in the next section. Note that the high computational
complexity of the DNN learning stage can be implemented
ofﬂine for a ﬁnite number of episodes at the central control unit.
IV. NUMERICAL RESULTS
A. Parameter Settings
This section offers simulation experiments to conﬁrm the
devised HTD3-based secure communication scheme for the
hybrid RIS-enhanced V2X network. Unless otherwise stated,
the RSU is designed with an uniform linear array (ULA) of
N
t
= 16 transmit and N
r
= 8 receive antennas. We assume
that the power budget of RSU P
R
max
ranges from 16 dBm to
35 dBm, the transmit power of DVU P
D
max
varies from 16 dBm
to 25 dBm, the number of hybrid RIS units N
I
ranges from 30
to 60, the outdated CSI factor ρ
d
ranges between 0.6 and 1 for
various simulation settings. We set the path loss exponent of
the RSU-CVU link as α
Rm
= 3, since this channel has plenty
of obstacles and scatterers
[15]. For the channel between the
RSU and the RIS and that between the RIS and the CVU, LoS
components exist in practical implementation. The parameter
α
RI
= α
Im
= 2 is used to signify the path loss exponent of the
RSU-RIS and RIS-CVU links. Similarly, the path loss exponents
of the RSU-Eve and RIS-Eve links are assumed to be α
RE
= 3
Fig. 3. Convergence property of different DRL-based techniques versus
episodes.
and α
IE
= 2, respectively. Rician fading factor κ = 3dB for all
the LoS cases is considered.
Regarding the proposed hierarchical learning framework, the
DQN contains 2-fully connected hidden layers with neurons
128 and 64, respectively. Adam optimizer is employed to train
neural network weights. As for TD3, the actor networks and
the critic networks are designed by using 2-layer networks with
sizes 256 and 128, respectively. For the actor network, tanh(·) is
considered as the activation function to bound the actions. Main
system simulation parameters are given in Table
I. Furthermore,
we compare our HTD3-based joint beamforming optimization
and spectrum allocation technique with the following methods:
r
The hierarchical DDPG (HDDPG)
[49] and variable rate
HDDPG
[50] approaches, which are used to design the
secure beamforming and allocate the spectrum in RIS-
enhanced vehicular networks.
r
The improved DQN technique
[31], where DNN is utilized
to calculate the Q-value function, when selecting the secure
communication strategy corresponding to the greatest Q-
value.
r
The scheme adopts successive convex approximation
(SCA) technique to tackle the secure communication prob-
lem
(22), and alternate optimization (AO) procedure is
employed to design transmit beamforming and allocate
spectrum resource
[15].
r
The transmit beamforming of RSU and the reﬂection
weights of hybrid RIS are designed without spectrum
sharing scheme (beamforming design only).
B. Convergence
First, the convergence property of various DRL-based ap-
proaches is compared, considering N
I
= 30,P
R
max
= 25 dBm
and P
D
max
= 18 dBm. The reward with increasing learning
episodes to explore the convergence performance of the devised
scheme and three compared techniques is presented in Fig.
3.
Obviously, our hierarchical training scheme realizes the better
performance than that of the HDDPG
[49] and self-adjusting
HDDPG
[50] techniques. Particularly, the proposed HTD3-
based scheme depends on the double Q-learning and smoothing
regularization techniques to reduce the overestimation bias and
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on September 09,2025 at 07:02:22 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
9234 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON VEHICULAR TECHNOLOGY, VOL. 74, NO. 6, JUNE 2025
TAB LE I
SIMULATION PARAMETERS
Fig. 4. Sum secrecy rate of all V2I links with varying average vehicle speed.
improve the network stable, and the enhanced policy is obtained
after a certain amount of learning episodes. The self-adjusting
HDDPG approach is able to attain the higher convergence rate by
exploiting variable rate learning strategy, but it obtains inferior
performance than that of the HTD3 scheme, producing the
smaller reward value. Furthermore, as expected, even though
the DQN-based training
[31] scheme has the relatively simple
architecture, the worst capability fails to improve the system
performance with improving learning episodes.
As presented in Fig.
3, the reward is enhanced as the number
of iterations increases, verifying the usefulness of the devised
hierarchical training method. When the iteration number reaches
about 45, the reward progressively converges in spite of small
ﬂuctuations owing to channel variation as well as the ε-greedy
strategy in the learning procedure. Despite the hybrid RIS-
enhanced V2X network is dynamic and the system state is large,
the proposed scheme only needs approximately 50 iterations to
appropriately converge.
We study the sum secrecy rate of all V2I links against the
average vehicle speed in Fig.
4. As expected, the sum V2I
secrecy rate attained by all approaches decreases as the average
speed increases, and the secrecy performance of decrease of
the proposed strategy is lower than that of the counterparts.
Moreover, when the power budget of RSU increases, the V2I
secrecy rate increases accordingly. The results are ascribed to the
dominant reason. A higher speed leads to sparser trafﬁc based
on the considered vehicular network setup, which increases the
distance between vehicles. To ensure the reliability of the active
V2V links, DVUs will increase the power budget to offset for
Fig. 5. Sum secrecy rate of all V2I links with varying Rician factor.
Fig. 6. Sum V2I secrecy rate with different number of active V2V links.
the higher path loss induced by the increased distance between
vehicles, which would produce stronger disturbance to the CVUs
and hence limits the secrecy performance improvement of V2I
links. The effect of various Rician factors on the secure perfor-
mance is depicted in Fig.
5. The sum V2I secrecy rate of all
approaches enhances as the value of κ increases. The result was
expected since all links tend to be more deterministic as Rician
factor increases, and a larger proportion of LoS components is
achieved to improve the secure communication performance.
To evaluate the effectiveness of spectrum reuse scheme on
the secure communication performance for the hybrid RIS-
enhanced V2X network, the requirement

m∈M
ρ
ml
≤ 1, ∀l
is ignored, which implies that the spectrum utilized by each V2I
link is coexisted by multiple active V2V links. From Fig.
6,
the sum V2I secrecy rate generated by three different methods
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on September 09,2025 at 07:02:22 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
YAO et al.: OPTIMIZING HYBRID RIS-AIDED ISAC SYSTEMS IN V2X NETWORKS 9235
Fig. 7. Performance comparisons for different radar MI threshold. (a) com-
munication secrecy rate, (b) communication data rate.
reduces as the number of spectrum sharing active V2V links
increases. The principal reasons for secure performance deteri-
oration are as follows. All V2I links are required to share channel
with several V2V links at the same time. To ensure the validity
of active V2V links, the power budget of the CVUs should be
regulated to minimize the disturbance to active V2V links. The
reduced power budget of all V2I links results in the degeneration
of the sum V2I secrecy rate. Moreover, an increase in number of
active V2V links will cause more disturbance to the available V2I
links and thus decrease SINR
C
m
value. When the power budget
of RSU is increased from 16 dBm to 27 dBm, the V2I secrecy
performance increases accordingly. If the condition is satisﬁed
L/M ≥ 3, the secure performance improvement is marginal.
We can observe that in this case, the available V2I links suffer
serious disturbance from active V2V links, which is the principal
reason that adversely inﬂuences the secure communication.
The sum secrecy rate and data rate of all V2I links against
the radar MI level for P
R
max
= 25 dBm and P
D
max
= 18 dBm,
are shown in Fig.
7 for ﬁve different approaches. From Fig. 7,
both the sum secrecy rate and data rate decrease as the radar
MI threshold increases for all techniques. That is because as
the radar detection performance demand increases, the hybrid
RIS-enhanced RSU takes more energy to focus the beamformer
of ISAC signals at the direction of the malicious target, and
hence less power is utilized for the communication waveform
transmission toward the direction of the connected CVUs. The
devised HTD3 technique provides higher sum secrecy rate and
Fig. 8. Radar MI value versus the number of RSU antennas.
Fig. 9. Radar MI versus different maximum tolerable outage probability.
data rate for various radar MI values as compared to the coun-
terparts.
We explore the inﬂuence of number of RSU antennas for
radar MI performance under different outdated CSI factor with
P
RIS
max
= 20 dBm and N
I
= 60. From Fig.
8, the radar MI value
increases as the increase of the number of RSU antennas. The
phenomenon is owing to the additional spatial degree of free-
doms (DoFs) introduced by the more RSU antennas, and then
improve the beamformer resolution. Moreover, the RIS-related
CSI error inﬂuences the sensing capability from two aspects: on
the one hand, each DVU requires more energy to satisfy the data
rate constraints for a higher CSI error level; on the other hand, the
MI degeneration is caused by the sensing part to address worse
RSU-RIS channel condition. Fig.
9 illustrates that the radar MI
value increases with the maximum tolerable outage probability
P
outage
max
increases. The power budget of the hybrid RIS is as-
sumed as P
RIS
max
∈{5dBm, 10 dBm, 20 dBm}. With the increase
of P
outage
max
, more relaxed constraint is imposed on the data rate
of V2V links, and less power is required on the communication
part to satisfy the transmission rate constrained, hence improve
the sensing performance. Furthermore, the sensing performance
improves with increasing the power consumption of the hybrid
RIS P
RIS
max
.
Fig.
10 illustrates the radar MI level versus the number
of active units on the hybrid RIS A of the proposed method
for P
RIS
max
∈{5dBm, 10 dBm, 20 dBm}, N
I
= 60 and optimized
passive RIS baseline is also considered as a benchmark. When
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on September 09,2025 at 07:02:22 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
9236 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON VEHICULAR TECHNOLOGY, VOL. 74, NO. 6, JUNE 2025
Fig. 10. Radar MI value versus the number of active units on hybrid RIS for
P
RIS
max
∈{5dBm, 10 dBm, 20 dBm}.
Fig. 11. Performance comparisons for different outdated CSI factor. (a) com-
munication secrecy rate, (b) communication data rate.
A = 0, the proposed approach is reduced to the passive RIS,
thus they have the similar radar MI performance. In addition,
the radar MI performance of our approach improves as the
value of A increases. The result indicates that the hybrid RIS
strategy dramatically enhances the radar sensing performance by
introducing a small amount of active elements to conventional
passive RIS.
Then, we evaluate how the sum secrecy rate and data rate are
inﬂuenced by the outdated channel factor ρ
d
in the considered
V2X network, that is, from ρ
d
= 0.5 to 1, when N
I
= 30,
P
R
max
= 25 dBm, and P
D
max
= 18 dBm. With the decrease of ρ
d
value, the transmission channel gets more outdated as described
in
(5), and ρ
d
= 1 indicates perfect CSI. From Fig. 11, when
channel gets more outdated (as the value of ρ
d
reduces), the sum
secrecy rate and data rate decrease for all optimization methods.
This is because a greater ρ
d
value signiﬁes more precise CSI,
which would enable all methods to optimize spectrum allocation
and secure beamforming to realize higher sum secrecy rate and
data rate of all V2I links. We can observe that decreasing ρ
d
value has more inﬂuences on the performance gain improvement
of the counterparts while the HTD3 technique still keeps the
communication security and quality at satisfactory level, sug-
gesting that the counterparts are less robust to the ﬂuctuation of
communication channel and the robust of the devised technique.
In particular, our scheme attains the sum secrecy rate and data
rate enhancements of 14.28% and 12.41%, compared with the
HDDPG method when ρ
d
= 0.9. Furthermore, in comparison,
our method realizes the best communication security and quality
among all methods against environment uncertainty. This is due
to the fact that the devised method takes into account the time
variant channels and employs TD3-tranining to validly learn the
dynamic V2X network.
Fig.
12 illustrates the system secrecy rate, data rate and
radar MI versus the power budget of RSU P
R
max
, considering
P
D
max
= 18 dBm, N
I
= 30 and ρ = 0.7. From Figs.
12(a) and
(b), the system secrecy rate and data rate of ﬁve different meth-
ods increase with increasing P
R
max
. As expected, when P
R
max
increases, the SINR at each CVU increases, resulting in the com-
munication performance enhancement. Furthermore, as we can
see, our training scheme outperforms the AO method. Indeed,
our learning strategy optimizes the secure beamforming and
allocates the spectrum resource, which synchronously facilitates
higher channel transmission beneﬁt for all CVUs and disturb
Eve, while the AO baseline method designs the secure beam-
forming in an alternating optimization manner. The HTD3-based
technique has superior performance than HDDPG and DQN in
terms of system secrecy rate and data rate. This is due to the fact
that delayed policy update scheme is used to reduce the function
approximation errors and thus ensure efﬁcient learning capacity.
As expected, the proposed hierarchical learning scheme offers
noteworthy higher secrecy rate and data rate than the hybrid
RIS-enhanced V2X network without spectrum allocation strat-
egy. At the same time, the radar MI level versus P
R
max
for various
methods is displayed in Fig.
12(c), which demonstrates that
the target detection performance becomes more excellent, as
P
R
max
increases. The results show that our scheme can effectively
ensure secure communication and radar sensing requirement via
joint beamforming optimization and spectrum allocation.
The sum secrecy rate, data rate and radar MI of all tech-
niques are investigated varying the reﬂection elements, namely,
from N
I
= 30 to 60, considering ρ
d
= 0.7, P
R
max
= 25 dBm
and P
D
max
= 20 dBm. As shown in Fig.
13(a) and (b),thesum
secrecy rate and data rate of all hybrid RIS-enhanced optimiza-
tion approaches prominently increase with the value of N
I
.As
expected, more hybrid reﬂection units, more transmission power
and propagation paths are reﬂected by the hybrid RIS to enhance
the received SINR at the CVUs but to deteriorate the received
SINR at the Eve. Moreover, the sum secrecy rate of the devised
training method is higher than that of the AO and DQN methods,
particularly, the performance gap evidently increases with N
I
,
this is because that with more hybrid RIS units, the HTD3-based
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on September 09,2025 at 07:02:22 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
YAO et al.: OPTIMIZING HYBRID RIS-AIDED ISAC SYSTEMS IN V2X NETWORKS 9237
Fig. 12. Performance comparisons versus the power budget of RSU. (a) communication secrecy rate, (b) communication data rate, (c) radar MI level.
Fig. 13. Performance comparisons versus the number of hybrid reﬂection elements. (a) communication secrecy rate, (b) communication data rate, (c) radar MI
level.
secure transmission technique gets more ﬂexible for reﬂection
beamforming optimization and thus realizes greater perfor-
mance improvements. Similarly, from Fig.
13(c), the radar MI
level increases with the increasing of N
I
as more hybrid RIS units
are capable of offering reﬂection beamforming gain to improve
target detection capability. Speciﬁcally, compared to the hybrid
RIS-enhanced method with 30-element, it can be found that a
60-element hybrid RIS provides about 0.8 b radar MI gain, since
the employment of hybrid RIS produces more signal paths and
enables extra DoFs for enhancing the radar detection capability.
Finally, to further demonstrate the advantages of the MI
metric, Fig.
14(a) illustrates the root mean square error (RMSE)
of target angle estimation for various radar performance metrics
versus the radar MI which is deﬁned in
(17). Here, we consider
R
m
min
= 5 bps/Hz for all V2I links, and employ the maximum
likelihood estimation (MLE) to estimate the angle of the ma-
licious eavesdropping target. We can observe that when radar
MI level is high, the RMSE of the proposed scheme is better
than that of the techniques based on the beampattern metric
[2]
and the MSE metric [51], but slightly worse than that of the
scheme based on the Cramer-Rao bound (CRB) metric [42].On
the contrary, at low radar MI level (below 0.9 dB), the RMSE
of the method based on the MI metric outperforms the other
three benchmark techniques. The result further conﬁrms that
the ISAC secure scheme based on the MI metric can obtain
superior radar sensing capability. Fig.
14(b) shows the sensing
RMSE versus the radar MI level. We can observe from the
curves that the RMSE of the proposed scheme is reduced with
the increase of radar MI value. On the other hand, when we
gradually reduce the data rate threshold of the V2I link from
Fig. 14. RMSE of target angle estimation versus the radar MI level. (a) various
radar performance metrics for R
m
min
= 5 bps/Hz, (b) various transmission rate
threshold R
m
min
.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on September 09,2025 at 07:02:22 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
9238 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON VEHICULAR TECHNOLOGY, VOL. 74, NO. 6, JUNE 2025
10 bps/Hz to 0 bps/Hz, corresponding to the four curves in the
ﬁgures, the considered system is accordingly converted from
communication-dominated to sensing-dominated. As a result, it
is observed that the radar sensing performance becomes better
gradually.
V. C
ONCLUSION
This paper proposed an optimization technique for securing
the hybrid active-passive RIS-aided V2X network, which in-
volved the joint design of the transmit beamforming of RSU,
the spectrum reuse scheme of V2X links, the active and pas-
sive reﬂection weights of hybrid RIS. The joint design was
formulated as an optimization problem to maximize the sum
V2I secrecy capacity subject to radar sensing performance and
overall power budget constraints. Since the system model is
dynamic, an efﬁcient HTD3 learning algorithm has been de-
veloped, where DQN technique has been integrated into the
TD3 procedure in a hierarchical framework for obtaining the
optimal anti-eavesdropping policy. The simulation results have
conﬁrmed the noteworthy improvement of the sum V2I secrecy
capacity while ensuring the V2V communication quality re-
quirement and desired RSU target detection performance under
a particular resource constraint.
R

Yu Yao (Member, IEEE) received the M.S. and Ph.D.
degrees in information and communication engineer-
ing from Southeast University, Nanjing, China, in
2010 and 2015, respectively. He is currently a Pro-
fessor with the School of Information and Com-
munication Engineering, Hainan University, Haikou,
China. From 2019 to 2020, he is a visiting Scholar
with the Department of Electrical Engineering and
Information Technology, University of Naples Fed-
erico II, Naples, Italy. His research interests include
reconﬁgurable intelligent surface integrated sensing
and communication physical layer security, and machine learning for wireless
communications.
Zhixing Zhu received the B.S. degree from Nan-
chang University, Nanchang, China, in 2022. He
is currently working toward the M.S. degree with
the School of Information Engineering, East China
Jiaotong University, Nanchang, China. His research
interests include reconﬁgurable intelligent surface
cooperative communications, physical layer security,
and machine learning for wireless communications.
Pu Miao (Member, IEEE) received the B.E. degree in
electronic engineering and information science from
Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, China, in 2010,
and the Ph.D. degree in electronic and computer en-
gineering from Southeast University, Nanjing, China
in 2015. He is currently with the School of Electronic
and Information Engineering, Qingdao University,
Qingdao, China. His research interests include image
processing and deep learning techniques for computer
vision.
Xu Cheng (Senior Member, IEEE) received the B.Sc.
degree in communication engineering, the M.Sc. and
the Ph.D. degrees in information and communication
engineering from the National University of Defense
Technology, Changsha, China in 2009, 2011 and 2016
respectively. From 2014 to 2015, he was a visiting
Student with the University of Naples “Federico II”,
Naples, Italy. From June 2016 to now, He has be-
ing working as an Assistant Professor with Policer
College of PAP, Chengdu, China. He is currently a
Postdoctoral position with the School of Electronics
and Communication Engineering, Shenzhen Campus of Sun Yat-sen University,
Shenzhen, China. His research interests include statistical signal processing,
with emphasis on the application of radar systems and wireless sensor networks.
Feng Shu (Member, IEEE) received the B.S. de-
gree from Fuyang Teachers College, Fuyang, China
in 1994, the M.S.degree from Xidian University,
Xian, China in 1997, and the Ph.D. degree from
Southeast University, Nanjing, China, in 2002. From
September 2009 to September 2010, he was a Vis-
iting Post-Doctor with the University of Texas at
Dallas, Richardson, TX, USA. From October 2005
to November 2020, he was with the School of Elec-
tronic and Optical Engineering, Nanjing University
of Science and Technology, Nanjing. Since December
2020, he has been with the School of Information and Communication Engineer-
ing, Hainan University, Haikou, China, where he is currently a Professor and the
Supervisor of the Ph.D. and graduate students. He has authored or coauthored
more than 300 archival journals with more than 100 articles on IEEE journals
and 170 SCI-indexed papers. He holds 16 Chinese patents. His research interests
include wireless networks, wireless location, and array signal processing. He
was the recipient of the Leading-Talent Plan of Hainan Province, the Mingjian
Scholar Chair Professor, and the Fujian Hundred-Talent Plan in Fujian Province.
Jiangzhou Wang (Fellow, IEEE) is currently a Pro-
fessor with the University of Kent, Canterbury, U.K.
He has authored or coauthored more than 400 papers
and four books. His research focuses on mobile com-
munications. He is a Fellow of the Royal Academy
of Engineering, U.K., and the IET. He was a recipient
of the 2022 IEEE Communications Society Leonard
G. Abraham Prize. He was the Technical Program
Chair of the 2019 IEEE International Conference on
Communications (ICC2019), Shanghai, the Execu-
tive Chair of the IEEE ICC2015, London, and the
Technical Program Chair of the IEEE WCNC2013.
